title: Founders do not need more ideas. They need better filters.
body:
Most founders are not stuck because they lack ideas. They are stuck because they keep feeding time into the wrong ones.

Product Strategy 25 is built to force sharper decisions earlier so you waste less time building things no one wants.

If your product backlog feels like a junk drawer, this is for you.

Good systems beat mood every time.

title: If your roadmap feels like a coping mechanism, fix the system.
body:
Product Strategy 25 is for the moment when you realize your team is not actually short on effort. You are short on clarity.

The right framework saves more time than the right hype.

That is the whole pitch.

title: How I Turn One Idea Into 10 Posts Without AI Slop
body:
## The Problem

I used to treat ideas like single-use objects.

One idea -> one post -> done.

Then I'd sit there the next day like:

> "cool... now what?"

Repeat that enough times and you end up doing one of two things:

- overthinking everything
- or not posting at all

Neither one scales.

## Where My Ideas Actually Come From

Nothing fancy.

- random thoughts
- things I've built
- frustrations
- "this sucks, there should be a better way"

That last one is the goldmine.

If I feel friction, odds are someone else does too.

## What Changed

I stopped treating ideas like finished products.

Now I treat them like **raw input**.

And instead of asking:

> "is this a good post?"

I ask:

> "how many angles can I squeeze out of this?"

## The Actual System (Not Pretty, But It Works)

### Step 1: Rant first

I don't start structured.

I just dump everything:

- what's annoying me
- what I think is broken
- what I wish existed

No filter. No polish.

### Step 2: Extract angles

From one rant, I usually get:

- a blunt take
- a practical tip
- a story
- a system
- a question

Same idea. Different surfaces.

### Step 3: Map to platforms

Each angle becomes a different format:

- short punch -> social post
- structured idea -> Dev.to
- repeatable idea -> Pinterest
- casual version -> Facebook

Same core idea, different packaging.

### Step 4: Store everything (this is the real unlock)

The biggest change wasn't AI.

It was having a place to dump everything.

I built a simple system (DB + queue) where I can:

- drop ideas
- expand them later
- schedule without thinking

That removed the "what do I post today?" problem entirely.

## Where AI Actually Helps (and Where It Doesn't)

I use AI a lot.

Probably too much.

### Where it helps:

- generating variations
- breaking writer's block
- speeding up structure

### Where it fails hard:

When you try to force *voice* out of it.

## What "AI Slop" Actually Is

AI slop isn't just bad writing.

It's this loop:

> you ask -> it gives something generic -> you tweak -> it gets worse -> repeat

You end up with content that:

- sounds like everyone else
- says nothing new
- feels safe and useless

Especially if you want something:

- edgy
- opinionated
- slightly unhinged

AI struggles there.

## The Fix

I stopped asking AI to create from scratch.

Now I:

1. start messy (my input)
2. use AI to expand, not invent
3. keep the tone mine

AI is a tool, not the source.

## The Real Outcome

This changed two things:

### 1. Consistency

I don't rely on "feeling like posting" anymore.

I already have:

- ideas
- drafts
- scheduled content

### 2. Output without burnout

Instead of forcing new ideas daily:

I reuse the same idea multiple times.

Which means:

- less pressure
- more content
- better ideas over time

## The Simple Version

If you do nothing else:

- stop writing one post per idea
- start extracting multiple angles
- store everything

That alone will outpace most people.

## Final Thought

You don't need more ideas.

You need to stop wasting the ones you already have.

title: I Built a Social Posting Pipeline That Survives Bad Brain Days
body:
Most days, I don't lose to complexity.

I lose to... doing nothing.

Staring at the screen.
Scrolling.
Opening GPT 50 times hoping something sticks.

So I stopped trying to "be motivated" and built a system that works even when I'm not.

## The Problem

My workflow used to be:

- have idea
- overthink idea
- maybe write post
- forget to publish
- repeat in 3 days

Zero consistency.
Zero reliability.
Lots of "I'll get to it later."

## The System (What Actually Runs)

Right now my setup is not fancy. It's just... layered correctly.

- **SQLite = source of truth**
- **JSON = mirror / fallback**
- **Worker = handles posting**
- **Playwright = fakes being me on platforms**
- **UI = manual override when things break**

That's it.

No magic. Just separation of responsibilities.

### Core flow

1. I dump content into a queue
2. It gets stored in SQLite
3. Worker runs every ~30 minutes
4. It tries to post
5. If it fails -> it stays in the system
6. I retry or reschedule later

No disappearing posts. No guessing.

## Why This Changed Everything

Before this, every post required:

- remembering to post
- deciding what to post
- actually posting

Now?

I just:

> throw ideas into the system and let it deal with timing

The biggest shift wasn't AI.
It was this:

> **I separated creation from execution.**

Once that clicked, everything got easier.

## The "Bad Brain Day" Design

I didn't build this for productivity.

I built it for the days where I'm useless.

So the system assumes:

- I will forget
- I will avoid work
- I will break things
- platforms will randomly fail

And it handles that.

## Failure Is Expected (Not Exceptional)

Stuff breaks constantly.

- platform UI changes
- tokens die
- Playwright gets weird
- I push something dumb and break my own app

So instead of trying to prevent failure, I designed for it:

- failed posts don't disappear
- they stay in the queue
- they get marked `failed`
- I retry or move them

Nothing is wasted.

## The "Oh Shit" Fallback

When something fails:

- it gets marked failed automatically
- I either:
  - retry it
  - or bump it to another day

That's it.

No panic. No rebuilding.

Just... try again later.

## The Unexpected Benefit

The biggest win wasn't automation.

It was **mental load reduction**.

Before:

- every post was a decision

Now:

- posting is a background process

That frees me up to actually build things again.

## The Honest State of This System

This isn't some perfect SaaS.

- it works
- I break it sometimes
- then I fix it

That's the loop.

But it's still better than relying on motivation.

## If You Want to Steal This Idea

You don't need my exact stack.

You need this structure:

- one place to store content (DB)
- one process to execute (worker)
- one way to recover (retry system)

That's it.

Everything else is optional.

## Final Thought

Motivation didn't fix my consistency.

A queue did.

title: Most Founders Do Not Need More Ideas. They Need Better Filters
body:
Founders do not need more ideas. They need better filters.

A lot of builders are not blocked because they lack creativity.

They are blocked because every idea feels plausible for just long enough to steal a week.

That is how backlogs turn into junk drawers.

You start with ambition:
- new feature ideas
- growth experiments
- AI angles
- platform expansions
- side bets that might be big

Pretty soon, you are not building a strategy anymore. You are just collecting options.

## The real problem is idea inflation

Every new idea arrives with a story:
- this could unlock growth
- this could differentiate the product
- this could bring in a new audience
- this could be quick to ship

Without a filter, every idea gets treated like an opportunity instead of a cost.

That is where founders lose time.

## A better question

Instead of asking Is this a good idea, ask:
- Is this aligned with the current product goal?
- Does this solve a painful problem for the intended buyer?
- Is this more important than what is already in flight?
- What do we stop doing if we do this?
- What evidence do we have besides excitement?

Excitement is not strategy.

## Why this is hard

Filtering sounds obvious, but it gets harder when:
- you are building alone
- you have multiple product directions
- you can execute fast
- new tools make every idea feel easier than it really is

Speed makes weak prioritization more dangerous, not less.

You can waste a month very efficiently.

## What helped me

I started treating product decisions less like inspiration and more like a sorting problem.

Not every good idea belongs in the current season of the business.

Some ideas are:
- now
- later
- maybe
- not for this audience
- not worth the maintenance cost

That framing removes a lot of guilt. You do not need to kill every idea. You just need to stop letting all of them compete for today.

## Final thought

The founders I trust most are not the ones with the most ideas.

They are the ones who can explain why they are ignoring ten attractive ones in order to finish one thing that matters.

That is the skill.

If you want a sharper way to evaluate, sort, and pressure-test product ideas, I made this for exactly that problem:
https://fleurdevie.gumroad.com/l/product-strategy-25

title: The Creator Block Debugger: Still Fixing It But It Works
body:
Creator block is usually not a talent problem. It is a starting problem.

I built a system that works when motivation is zero. Here is how:

1. Have a queue of 30 ready-to-post pieces
2. Use prompts instead of waiting for ideas
3. Batch create when feeling creative
4. Schedule and forget

The goal is to never face a blank page.

title: 100 Prompts in 30 Days - Here is What Happened
body:
I used the same prompt pack for 30 days straight. Results:

- Posted every day without fail
- 3x my normal output
- Actually started enjoying creating again

The prompts were not magic. They were starting points. That is the whole point.

title: One Prompt Can Unblock An Entire Content Week
body:
Most creators wait for inspiration. The ones who succeed have systems.

A single well-crafted prompt can give you 10 angles, 10 titles, 10 post ideas.

Stop waiting. Start prompting.

title: Good Systems Beat Mood Every Time
body:
I used to only create when inspired. Then I built a system that posts while I sleep.

Results: No more posting from desperation. No more showing up exhausted.

Build the system. Let it work.

title: How I Turn One Idea Into 10 Posts Without AI Slop
body:
Repurposing is not copying. It is multiplying.

One idea becomes:
- A short post
- A thread
- A newsletter
- A video script

The key is having a system, not just hoping for more ideas.
